content: {"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://www.ise.fraunhofer.de/content/dam/ise/de/documents/publications/conference-paper/38th-eupvsec-2021/Oez_4AV15.pdf", "source_title": "The Impact of the Lamination Process on the Adhesion Properties at the Glass-Encapsulant Interface and Damp Heat Stability of PV Modules", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.85, "year": 2021, "authors": ["Oez AK", "Herzog C", "Wellens C", "Mansour DE", "Heinrich M", "Kraft A"], "venue": "38th EU PVSEC", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "Relative maintained peel force at the glass-encapsulant interface after 3000 hours of damp heat at 85 C/85% RH varied from 15.7% to 73.0% depending on encapsulant material and lamination process.", "direct_quote": "The peel test results show that the relative maintained peel force (RMPF) varies in the range from 15.7-73.0% depending on the material and the lamination process. The influences of the lamination process on the adhesion properties under water vapour ingress were investigated by exposing the modules to the damp heat test (85 C, 85% r.h.) for 1500 h and 3000 h", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.95, "confidence": 0.93, "perspective": "Scientific", "entities": ["RMPF", "peel force", "15.7-73.0%", "damp heat", "85 C", "85% RH", "3000 hours"]}, {"statement": "EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE samples, and higher lamination temperature was beneficial for peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants.", "direct_quote": "EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after DH than POE samples and a higher lamination temperature was beneficial in peel force and gel content before and after ageing for cross-linking encapsulants such as EVA and POE. For the used TPO the peel force did not change for different lamination temperatures", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.92, "confidence": 0.91, "perspective": "Industry", "entities": ["EVA", "POE", "TPO", "peel force", "lamination temperature", "crosslinking"]}, {"statement": "TPO with PET/Al/PET/Olefin backsheet had peel forces around 160 N/cm before damp heat but dropped to 33-45 N/cm after 3000 hours DH, the most severe absolute degradation observed.", "direct_quote": "the highest peel force degradation occurred at the laminates which used the PET/Aluminium/PET/Olefin backsheet with a TPO encapsulant. While these laminates had peel forces of around 160 N/cm before the damp heat exposure, they dropped to 33-45 N/cm after exposing them to DH for 3000 hours", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.94, "perspective": "Industry", "entities": ["TPO", "160 N/cm", "33-45 N/cm", "3000 hours DH", "PET/Al/PET/Olefin"]}, {"statement": "PET/PET/Primer backsheet laminates dropped from around 120 N/cm to 18-25 N/cm peel force after 3000 hours DH, while polypropylene backsheet laminates degraded less from about 65 N/cm to 30 N/cm.", "direct_quote": "laminates with the PET/PET/Primer backsheet dropped from around 120 N/cm to 18-25 N/cm after DH 3000. The laminates with polypropylene backsheet had much lower peel forces (~65 N/cm) before DH testing compared to the other two backsheets, but after the damp heat exposure they degraded less than the others and their peel forces dropped to ~30 N/cm", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.88, "confidence": 0.93, "perspective": "Scientific", "entities": ["PET/PET/Primer", "120 N/cm", "18-25 N/cm", "polypropylene", "65 N/cm", "30 N/cm"]}, {"statement": "Peel tests were conducted per BS EN ISO 8510 standard at 90-degree angle with 10 mm wide strips at 50 mm/min at the glass-encapsulant interface.", "direct_quote": "The peel force at the glass-encapsulant interface is measured with the PSE peel-off instrument at an angle of 90 degrees. The peel tests were conducted at the centre of the small-scale laminates according to the BS EN ISO 8510 standards by cutting strips with the width of 10 mm and length of 200 mm. These strips were peeled off with a speed of 50 mm/min at room temperature", "fact_category": "standard_reference", "relevance_score": 0.68, "confidence": 0.95, "perspective": "Regulatory", "entities": ["BS EN ISO 8510", "90 degree peel test", "10 mm strips", "50 mm/min"]}, {"statement": "Lamination at 180 C for 4 minutes produced the highest peel forces for EVA and POE despite shorter curing, while 160 C for 10 minutes gave slightly higher gel content.", "direct_quote": "The peel forces for lamination temperatures of 180 C are the highest even with shorter lamination times. For the un-aged laminates, it was found that the gel content of the 160 C laminates was slightly higher than the ones laminated at 180 C. This happened due to the lower lamination time at 180 C. The 160 C laminates had a curing time of 10 minutes, while the 180 C laminates had a curing time of 4 minutes", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.75, "confidence": 0.92, "perspective": "Industry", "entities": ["180 C", "4 minutes", "160 C", "10 minutes", "gel content", "peel force"]}], "evidence_summary": "Fraunhofer ISE study with quantitative peel force data for EVA/POE/TPO under 3000h damp heat (85C/85%RH). RMPF 15.7-73.0%. TPO dropped from 160 to 33-45 N/cm. Higher lamination temperature improves adhesion."}, {"source_url": "https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/articles/degradation-study-peel-strength-mini-modules-under-damp-heat-condition", "source_title": "Degradation Study of the Peel Strength of Mini-Modules Under Damp Heat Condition", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.73, "overall_relevance": 0.8, "year": 0, "authors": ["Wu D", "Zhu J", "Betts T", "Gottschalg R"], "venue": "CREST / Loughborough University", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "Activation energy for adhesion degradation between backsheet and encapsulant under damp heat was approximately 51 kJ/mol from Arrhenius analysis at 85% RH with temperatures of 95, 85, and 65 C.", "direct_quote": "Ln RD vs. 1/T plot is shown at constant RH of 85% but varying T of 95 C, 85 C and 65 C. A linear line is observable, allowing Ea to be calculated: Ea is approximately 51 KJ/mol. An empirical kinetic model is developed by assuming that the rate of adhesion degradation is proportional to moisture concentration at the interface", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.87, "confidence": 0.88, "perspective": "Scientific", "entities": ["activation energy", "51 kJ/mol", "Arrhenius", "85% RH", "95 C", "85 C", "65 C"]}, {"statement": "Rate of adhesion degradation is proportional to moisture concentration at the interface with Arrhenius temperature dependence, and temperature acceleration is faster than relative humidity acceleration.", "direct_quote": "An empirical kinetic model is developed by assuming that the rate of adhesion degradation is proportional to moisture concentration at the interface of backsheet/encapsulant and the reaction rate constant is Arrhenius dependent. Both T and RH are accelerators of the degradation. The rate of T acceleration is faster than that of RH", "fact_category": "causal_link", "relevance_score": 0.83, "confidence": 0.87, "perspective": "Scientific", "entities": ["kinetic model", "Arrhenius", "moisture concentration", "temperature acceleration"]}, {"statement": "Acceleration factors of 3-6 times in testing time were achieved at higher stress conditions compared to baseline of 65 C and 85% RH for adhesion strength degradation.", "direct_quote": "Acceleration factors around 3-6 in testing time is achieved for the other three conditions compared with that at 65 C T and 85% RH. A concept of stress dose is developed for the quantification of accumulative stresses which is actually the right part of the above formula", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.78, "confidence": 0.86, "perspective": "Methodological", "entities": ["acceleration factor", "3-6x", "65 C", "85% RH", "stress dose"]}, {"statement": "Adhesion strength follows exponential decay model S = S0*exp(-t/t_char)^beta where degradation slope parameters depend on temperature and humidity stress levels.", "direct_quote": "S = S0 * exp(-t/t_char)^beta. Where S is adhesion strength at time t, S0 is the strength without degradation, beta and t_char are assumed to be function of stress levels having an influence on degradation slope. A linear relationship is obtained by plotting the changes of adhesion strength and temperature dose", "fact_category": "methodology", "relevance_score": 0.8, "confidence": 0.85, "perspective": "Methodological", "entities": ["adhesion strength model", "exponential decay", "stress dose"]}], "evidence_summary": "Loughborough/DOE study establishing kinetic model for adhesion degradation under damp heat with Ea=51 kJ/mol. Exponential decay model. Temperature acceleration 3-6x faster than humidity. Directly relevant to adhesion degradation prediction."}]}
